title: Safe Administration of HIGH-ALERT Intravenous Potassium Chloride for Symptomatic Hypokalemia in a 68-Year-Old on a Loop Diuretic and Digoxin: Recognizing Hypokalemia and Its ECG/Cardiac Effects, the Cardinal RULE That IV Potassium Is NEVER Pushed (Always Diluted and Pump-Controlled with Rate/Route Limits), Pre-Administration and Monitoring Safeguards, and Preventing Over-Correction
subtitle: POTASSIUM is the major intracellular cation and is tightly regulated because both LOW and HIGH levels are dangerous to the heart. HYPOKALEMIA (serum potassium below ~3.5 mEq/L) commonly results from LOOP or THIAZIDE DIURETICS, GI losses (vomiting, diarrhea, nasogastric suction), poor intake, intracellular SHIFTS (alkalosis, insulin, beta-agonists), hyperaldosteronism, and MAGNESIUM depletion (which makes hypokalemia REFRACTORY until magnesium is also replaced). EFFECTS: the most dangerous are CARDIAC — dysrhythmias and characteristic ECG changes (FLATTENED T waves, prominent U WAVES, ST-segment depression, and a prolonged QT) that can progress to life-threatening arrhythmias; hypokalemia also POTENTIATES DIGOXIN TOXICITY (a key danger in patients on both a diuretic and digoxin). Other features include muscle WEAKNESS, cramps, diminished reflexes, fatigue, decreased bowel motility/ileus, and polyuria. CORRECTION: when possible, the ORAL route is PREFERRED (safer); INTRAVENOUS replacement is reserved for SEVERE or SYMPTOMATIC hypokalemia or when oral is not tolerated — and IV potassium is a designated HIGH-ALERT MEDICATION (ISMP) whose misadministration can be FATAL. The CARDINAL SAFETY RULE: IV potassium is NEVER given by IV PUSH, bolus, or undiluted — a rapid potassium bolus causes fatal cardiac arrest/arrhythmia. It must ALWAYS be DILUTED and infused SLOWLY by an INFUSION PUMP. Concentrated potassium chloride vials are REMOVED from floor/unit stock (a national safety practice); commercially PREMIXED bags are used, and potassium is NOT added to a hanging/infusing IV bag (risk of an inadvertent bolus). RATE and ROUTE limits: through a PERIPHERAL line, the rate is typically limited to about 10 mEq/hr (with dilute concentrations, e.g., ~10 mEq/100 mL) because potassium is irritating to veins and causes pain/phlebitis; FASTER infusion (e.g., up to ~20 mEq/hr or more) requires a CENTRAL line WITH CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING in a monitored setting — never exceed the ordered rate, and always use a pump (never gravity/free-flow). PRE-ADMINISTRATION safeguards: verify the order and the serum potassium; ASSESS RENAL FUNCTION and URINE OUTPUT, because impaired kidneys/oliguria greatly raise the risk of dangerous HYPERKALEMIA ('do not give potassium without adequate renal function/urine output'); obtain a baseline ECG; check and replace MAGNESIUM if low; and perform an INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK of this high-alert drug (right drug, concentration, rate, pump settings) with barcode scanning and the rights of medication administration. DURING/AFTER administration: place high-rate infusions on a CARDIAC MONITOR; frequently assess the IV SITE for pain, burning, phlebitis, or infiltration (potassium is a vesicant-like irritant — slow/stop and reassess for site reactions); recheck the serum potassium; and watch for signs of OVER-CORRECTION to HYPERKALEMIA (peaked T waves, widening QRS, bradycardia, muscle weakness). PATIENT TEACHING: dietary potassium sources (bananas, oranges/citrus, potatoes, tomatoes, spinach, beans, dried fruit), the importance of follow-up labs, and reporting weakness, palpitations, or irregular heartbeat; for patients on diuretics, discuss potassium-sparing options and monitoring. NURSING ROLE: recognize hypokalemia and its cardiac/ECG effects; correct it SAFELY (oral preferred; IV NEVER pushed — always diluted, pump-controlled, within rate/route limits, with cardiac monitoring for high rates); assess renal function/urine output and magnesium first; perform the high-alert double-check; monitor the ECG, the IV site, and repeat levels; prevent over-correction; and educate the patient.

patient.name: Doris L.
patient.code: Full code; goal = safely correct symptomatic hypokalemia, administer high-alert IV potassium chloride without error, protect the heart, and prevent both under- and over-correction
patient.admitDate: A 68-year-old woman with heart failure and hypertension, maintained on furosemide (a loop diuretic) and digoxin, presents with several days of fatigue, generalized muscle WEAKNESS, leg cramps, and palpitations. Her serum potassium is 2.8 mEq/L (hypokalemia) and her ECG shows flattened T waves with prominent U waves and occasional premature beats. She is at particular risk because hypokalemia POTENTIATES digoxin toxicity. The provider orders intravenous potassium chloride replacement (plus magnesium check) because she is symptomatic. The nurse must administer this HIGH-ALERT medication safely — never as a push, always diluted and pump-controlled with appropriate rate/route limits, after assessing renal function and urine output, with cardiac and IV-site monitoring and prevention of over-correction.
patient.room: Telemetry/med-surg unit with continuous cardiac monitoring; IV potassium chloride replacement (premixed) via infusion pump; serial potassium and magnesium levels; interdisciplinary team (provider, bedside RN, pharmacy); monitoring for arrhythmia, digoxin toxicity, IV-site reaction, and over-correction

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; fatigue, generalized muscle WEAKNESS, leg cramps, and palpitations; ECG shows FLATTENED T waves with prominent U WAVES and ST depression — serum potassium 2.8 mEq/L (hypokalemia); on furosemide + digoxin (hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity)

37.0°C; before IV potassium: verified the order + serum K; confirmed adequate RENAL FUNCTION and URINE OUTPUT; baseline ECG obtained; MAGNESIUM checked/replaced (low Mg makes hypokalemia refractory); independent DOUBLE-CHECK of the high-alert premixed bag (drug/concentration/rate/pump)

37.0°C; COMMERCIALLY PREMIXED, DILUTED potassium chloride infused via INFUSION PUMP — NEVER pushed/bolused/undiluted; peripheral rate limited (~10 mEq/hr) for vein tolerance; continuous CARDIAC MONITORING; not added to a hanging bag; rights of medication administration + barcode verified

37.0°C; during the infusion: continuous ECG, frequent IV-SITE checks for pain/burning/phlebitis/infiltration (potassium is irritating — slow/stop and reassess if reaction), and a planned recheck of serum potassium; watching for over-correction

37.0°C; WARNING pattern of over-correction to HYPERKALEMIA if given too fast/too much or with poor renal function — PEAKED T waves, widening QRS, bradycardia, and muscle weakness; would require stopping the infusion and treating hyperkalemia — underscoring why rate limits, renal assessment, and monitoring matter

37.0°C; potassium safely corrected to 4.0 mEq/L; ECG normalized (T waves restored, U waves resolved), symptoms improved, no arrhythmia or digoxin toxicity; educated on dietary potassium and diuretic monitoring; IV site intact

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing hypokalemia (causes + cardiac/ECG effects + digoxin link)
labs.v: Doris L., 68, with heart failure/hypertension on FUROSEMIDE (a loop diuretic) and DIGOXIN, presents with fatigue, generalized muscle WEAKNESS, leg cramps, and palpitations; her serum potassium is 2.8 mEq/L (HYPOKALEMIA, normal ~3.5-5.0) and her ECG shows FLATTENED T waves with prominent U WAVES, ST depression, and occasional premature beats. HYPOKALEMIA commonly results from DIURETICS (loop/thiazide), GI losses (vomiting/diarrhea/NG suction), poor intake, intracellular shifts (alkalosis/insulin/beta-agonists), hyperaldosteronism, and MAGNESIUM depletion. Its most dangerous effects are CARDIAC — dysrhythmias and the classic ECG changes above that can progress to lethal arrhythmias — and it POTENTIATES DIGOXIN TOXICITY, a key hazard for Doris who takes both a diuretic and digoxin. Other features: muscle weakness/cramps, decreased reflexes, fatigue, reduced bowel motility/ileus, and polyuria. Because she is symptomatic with a significantly low level, the provider orders IV potassium replacement (and a magnesium check). The nurse's central task is to recognize the hypokalemia and its cardiac risk and then administer the high-alert IV potassium SAFELY.
labs.r: HYPOKALEMIA (K <3.5) — causes: DIURETICS, GI losses, poor intake, intracellular shifts, hyperaldosteronism, low Mg. Effects: CARDIAC (FLAT T waves, U WAVES, ST depression, arrhythmias) — most dangerous; POTENTIATES DIGOXIN TOXICITY; muscle weakness/cramps, ↓reflexes, ileus, polyuria. Symptomatic/severe → IV replacement. RN role: recognize hypokalemia + its cardiac/ECG effects + digoxin risk.

labs.n: KCl is HIGH-ALERT — oral preferred; NEVER IV push; dilute + pump + rate/route limits + cardiac monitoring
labs.v: Intravenous potassium chloride is a designated HIGH-ALERT MEDICATION (ISMP) because misadministration can be FATAL. When feasible, the ORAL route is PREFERRED (safer and adequate for mild deficits); IV is reserved for SEVERE or SYMPTOMATIC hypokalemia or when oral is not tolerated. The CARDINAL RULE: IV potassium is NEVER given by IV PUSH, bolus, or undiluted — a rapid potassium bolus causes fatal cardiac arrest/arrhythmia. It is ALWAYS DILUTED and infused SLOWLY via an INFUSION PUMP (never by gravity/free-flow). Use commercially PREMIXED bags; do NOT add potassium to a hanging/infusing bag (risk of an inadvertent bolus), and concentrated KCl vials are removed from unit stock as a national safety measure. RATE/ROUTE limits protect the patient: via a PERIPHERAL line, typically limit to about 10 mEq/hr with a dilute concentration (e.g., ~10 mEq/100 mL) because potassium irritates veins and causes pain/phlebitis; FASTER infusion (e.g., up to ~20 mEq/hr or higher) requires a CENTRAL line WITH CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING in a monitored/ICU setting. Never exceed the ordered rate to 'catch up.' For Doris, a premixed, diluted bag is infused by pump at a peripheral-appropriate rate with telemetry monitoring.
labs.r: IV KCl = HIGH-ALERT; ORAL preferred when feasible; IV for severe/symptomatic. CARDINAL RULE: NEVER IV push/bolus/undiluted (fatal) — ALWAYS DILUTE + INFUSION PUMP. Use PREMIXED bags; don't add to a hanging bag; concentrated KCl off floor stock. PERIPHERAL ~10 mEq/hr (dilute, vein irritation); FASTER (~20 mEq/hr+) only CENTRAL line + CONTINUOUS cardiac monitoring. Never exceed ordered rate. RN role: administer IV KCl safely (never push, dilute, pump, rate/route limits).

labs.v: Before giving IV potassium, the nurse performs critical SAFEGUARDS: VERIFY the order and the current serum POTASSIUM level; ASSESS RENAL FUNCTION and URINE OUTPUT — adequate kidney function and urine output are essential because impaired kidneys/oliguria sharply increase the risk of dangerous HYPERKALEMIA (the classic teaching: do NOT administer potassium without confirming adequate renal function/urine output); obtain a BASELINE ECG given the cardiac risk; check and REPLACE MAGNESIUM if low, because hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia REFRACTORY to potassium replacement; and confirm IV access is patent and appropriate for the planned rate (central line for high rates). Because KCl is HIGH-ALERT, perform an INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK with a second nurse (verifying the right drug, the concentration/total dose, the rate, and the pump programming), use BARCODE scanning, and apply the rights of medication administration (right patient, drug, dose, route, time, documentation). The nurse also confirms the infusion is a premixed, diluted bag on a pump — not a concentrate to be pushed or added to a running line. These steps prevent the catastrophic errors historically associated with potassium.
labs.r: PRE-ADMIN safeguards: verify order + serum K; ASSESS RENAL FUNCTION + URINE OUTPUT (oliguria/renal failure → hyperkalemia risk — don't give K without adequate output); baseline ECG; check/replace MAGNESIUM (low Mg → refractory hypokalemia); confirm appropriate IV access/route. HIGH-ALERT → INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK (drug/concentration/rate/pump) + barcode + the rights. Confirm premixed/diluted on a pump (not a push/added concentrate). RN role: complete safety checks before administering.

labs.v: DURING and AFTER the infusion, the nurse MONITORS closely: for higher rates, keep the patient on CONTINUOUS CARDIAC MONITORING and watch the ECG; frequently assess the IV SITE for PAIN, burning, redness, phlebitis, or infiltration, because potassium is a vein irritant — if the site reacts, SLOW or STOP the infusion and reassess (a central line is preferred for higher concentrations/rates); RECHECK the serum potassium after replacement to guide further dosing; and assess for resolution of symptoms and ECG changes. Critically, WATCH FOR OVER-CORRECTION to HYPERKALEMIA — peaked T waves, widening QRS, bradycardia, and muscle weakness — which can occur with too-rapid/excessive replacement or impaired renal excretion; if hyperkalemia develops, STOP the potassium and treat (calcium, insulin/dextrose, etc.). Also ensure MAGNESIUM is corrected, since hypokalemia will not resolve while magnesium is low. Do NOT give a 'bolus to catch up,' do NOT increase the rate beyond the order, and do NOT bypass the pump. For Doris, telemetry showed restoration of normal T waves and resolution of U waves as the potassium normalized, with no arrhythmia, digoxin toxicity, or site reaction.
labs.r: MONITOR: continuous cardiac monitoring (high rates) + ECG; frequent IV-SITE checks (pain/burning/phlebitis/infiltration → slow/stop, central preferred); RECHECK serum K; assess symptom/ECG resolution. PREVENT OVER-CORRECTION → HYPERKALEMIA (peaked T, wide QRS, bradycardia, weakness) from too-fast/excessive replacement or renal impairment → STOP + treat. Correct MAGNESIUM (refractory hypokalemia). No bolus 'to catch up,' no exceeding ordered rate, no bypassing the pump. RN role: monitor ECG/site/levels + prevent over-correction.

labs.n: Nursing role + patient teaching; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PATIENT TEACHING: review dietary POTASSIUM sources (bananas, oranges/citrus, potatoes, tomatoes, spinach, beans, dried fruit, dairy) for at-risk patients, the importance of FOLLOW-UP LABS while on diuretics, and reporting WEAKNESS, PALPITATIONS, or an irregular heartbeat; for patients on loop/thiazide diuretics, discuss potassium monitoring and potassium-sparing strategies, and (given digoxin) the danger of hypokalemia-potentiated digoxin toxicity. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT give potassium by IV push/bolus or undiluted; did NOT add potassium to a hanging bag or bypass the infusion pump; did NOT exceed the ordered rate or run a high rate through a peripheral line without central access/monitoring; did NOT administer without confirming adequate renal function/urine output; did NOT skip the independent double-check; and did NOT ignore IV-site pain or signs of over-correction. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: recognition of hypokalemia and its cardiac risk; oral replacement when feasible; strict adherence to dilution/pump/rate/route rules; premixed bags with concentrated KCl off unit stock; pre-administration renal/urine and magnesium assessment; independent double-check and barcode scanning; cardiac and IV-site monitoring; recheck of levels; and prevention of over-correction. EXPECTED outcome: potassium is corrected safely to the normal range, the ECG and symptoms resolve, no arrhythmia or digoxin toxicity occurs, the IV site stays intact, and the patient understands prevention. NURSING ROLE: recognize hypokalemia and its effects; correct it SAFELY (oral preferred; IV never pushed — diluted, pump-controlled, within rate/route limits, with cardiac monitoring); assess renal function/urine output and magnesium first; perform the high-alert double-check; monitor ECG/site/levels; prevent over-correction; and educate.
labs.r: TEACH: dietary potassium (bananas/citrus/potatoes/tomatoes/spinach/beans), follow-up labs on diuretics, report weakness/palpitations/irregular HR; diuretic + digoxin risks. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no IV push/undiluted, no adding to a hanging bag/bypassing pump, no exceeding rate or high peripheral rate without central/monitoring, no giving without renal/urine check, no skipping double-check, didn't ignore site pain/over-correction. PROTECTIVE: recognition, oral when feasible, dilution/pump/rate rules, premixed bags, pre-assessment, double-check/barcode, ECG/site monitoring, recheck levels. RN role: recognize + administer safely + monitor + educate.

nursesNote: Telemetry/med-surg — Doris L., 68, HF/HTN on FUROSEMIDE + DIGOXIN. Fatigue, generalized muscle WEAKNESS, leg cramps, palpitations; ECG = FLATTENED T waves + prominent U WAVES + ST depression + occasional PVCs; serum K 2.8 (HYPOKALEMIA). HIGH RISK: hypokalemia POTENTIATES DIGOXIN TOXICITY. Hypokalemia causes: diuretics, GI losses, poor intake, intracellular shifts, low Mg; effects: cardiac (arrhythmias/ECG changes — most dangerous), weakness/cramps/↓reflexes, ileus, polyuria. Symptomatic + significantly low → IV potassium ordered (+ Mg check). IV KCl = HIGH-ALERT (ISMP) — ORAL preferred when feasible; IV for severe/symptomatic. CARDINAL RULE: NEVER IV push/bolus/undiluted (fatal arrest) — ALWAYS DILUTE + INFUSION PUMP; commercially PREMIXED bag; do NOT add to a hanging bag; concentrated KCl off unit stock. RATE/ROUTE: peripheral ~10 mEq/hr (dilute, vein irritation/phlebitis); faster (~20 mEq/hr+) ONLY central line + CONTINUOUS cardiac monitoring; never exceed ordered rate; never gravity/free-flow. PRE-ADMIN: verified order + K; confirmed adequate RENAL FUNCTION + URINE OUTPUT (oliguria/renal failure → hyperkalemia risk — 'no K without output'); baseline ECG; checked/replaced MAGNESIUM (low Mg → refractory hypokalemia); INDEPENDENT DOUBLE-CHECK of high-alert drug (drug/concentration/rate/pump) + barcode + rights. ADMINISTER: premixed diluted KCl via pump, peripheral rate-limited, telemetry on. MONITOR: continuous ECG; frequent IV-SITE checks (pain/burning/phlebitis/infiltration → slow/stop, central preferred); RECHECK serum K; watch OVER-CORRECTION → HYPERKALEMIA (peaked T, wide QRS, bradycardia, weakness → STOP + treat). TEACH: dietary potassium (bananas/citrus/potatoes/tomatoes/spinach/beans), follow-up labs on diuretics, report weakness/palpitations/irregular HR. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no IV push/undiluted, no adding to hanging bag/bypassing pump, no exceeding rate or high peripheral rate w/o central+monitoring, no giving without renal/urine check, no skipping double-check, didn't ignore site pain/over-correction. Outcome: K corrected to 4.0, ECG/symptoms resolved, no arrhythmia/digoxin toxicity, site intact.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: telemetry/med-surg with continuous cardiac monitoring; symptomatic hypokalemia (K 2.8) on furosemide + digoxin
DIAGNOSIS: hypokalemia — replace potassium safely; monitor for arrhythmia and digoxin toxicity
LABS/DIAGNOSTICS: serum potassium and magnesium now and after replacement; basic metabolic panel/renal function; baseline and ongoing ECG; digoxin level if toxicity suspected
Use oral potassium when feasible; IV potassium for severe/symptomatic hypokalemia
IV potassium chloride: commercially PREMIXED, diluted; infuse via infusion PUMP — NEVER IV push, bolus, undiluted, or by gravity; do NOT add potassium to a hanging IV bag
Peripheral line: limit to ~10 mEq/hr with dilute concentration; faster rates (e.g., up to ~20 mEq/hr) only via central line with continuous cardiac monitoring; do not exceed the ordered rate
Independent double-check of the high-alert medication (drug, concentration, rate, pump settings) with barcode scanning and the rights of medication administration
Confirm adequate renal function and urine output before administering potassium
Replace magnesium if low (hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory)
Continuous cardiac monitoring during higher-rate infusion; assess the IV site frequently for pain/burning/phlebitis/infiltration and slow/stop if a reaction occurs
Recheck serum potassium after replacement; watch for over-correction to hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, widening QRS, bradycardia, weakness) and stop/treat if it develops
EDUCATION: dietary potassium sources, the need for follow-up labs on diuretics, and reporting weakness, palpitations, or irregular heartbeat

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this patient with hypokalemia?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Serum potassium 2.8 mEq/L with fatigue, muscle weakness, cramps, and palpitations — symptomatic hypokalemia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — symptomatic, significant hypokalemia requiring correction. RN role: recognize hypokalemia.

opts.text: ECG changes of hypokalemia — flattened T waves, prominent U waves, ST depression, and premature beats — reflecting cardiac risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cardiac/ECG effects are the most dangerous feature. RN role: recognize ECG changes + arrhythmia risk.

opts.text: She takes furosemide (a cause of potassium loss) and digoxin — hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the diuretic explains the loss and the digoxin adds toxicity risk. RN role: connect the medications to the risk.

opts.text: IV potassium is a high-alert medication that must never be pushed — it must be diluted and pump-controlled within rate/route limits
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the cardinal safety rule for IV potassium. RN role: administer safely (never push).

opts.text: Assess renal function and urine output and check magnesium before replacing potassium
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — renal/urine status and magnesium are essential pre-administration safeguards. RN role: pre-administration assessment.

opts.text: Plan continuous cardiac monitoring, IV-site monitoring, a recheck of potassium, and vigilance for over-correction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — monitoring during/after protects against arrhythmia, site injury, and over-correction. RN role: monitor.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — documented before medication administration
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine before medications. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Because the level is dangerously low, give a rapid IV potassium push to correct it quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG and potentially fatal. IV potassium is NEVER pushed/bolused — a rapid bolus causes cardiac arrest; it must be diluted and infused slowly by pump.

opts.text: Administer the potassium without checking renal function or urine output to avoid delay
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Adequate renal function/urine output must be confirmed first; giving potassium with impaired kidneys/oliguria risks dangerous hyperkalemia.

opts.text: Hypokalemia commonly results from diuretics, GI losses, and magnesium depletion, and causes dangerous cardiac dysrhythmias and ECG changes (flat T waves, U waves)
opts.rat: CORRECT — causes and cardiac effects. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity, so a patient on a diuretic plus digoxin is at heightened risk
opts.rat: CORRECT — the digoxin interaction. Foundation for risk awareness.

opts.text: Oral potassium is preferred when feasible; IV potassium is a high-alert drug reserved for severe/symptomatic hypokalemia
opts.rat: CORRECT — route selection principle. Foundation for safe replacement.

opts.text: IV potassium is never pushed or given undiluted; it is always diluted and infused by pump, with peripheral rates limited (~10 mEq/hr) and faster rates only via a central line with cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — the cardinal administration rules. Foundation for preventing fatal errors.

opts.text: Renal function/urine output must be adequate before giving potassium, and magnesium must be corrected because low magnesium makes hypokalemia refractory
opts.rat: CORRECT — pre-administration safeguards. Foundation for safe, effective replacement.

opts.text: Over-correction can cause hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, widening QRS, bradycardia), so potassium is rechecked and the patient monitored
opts.rat: CORRECT — over-correction risk and monitoring. Foundation for ongoing safety.

opts.text: A rapid IV potassium bolus is an acceptable way to correct severe hypokalemia quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A potassium bolus/push is potentially fatal (cardiac arrest); potassium is always diluted and infused slowly by pump.

opts.text: Potassium can be safely added by the nurse to an already-hanging IV bag at the bedside
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Adding potassium to a hanging bag risks an inadvertent bolus and uneven concentration; use commercially premixed bags.

opts.text: Recognize the symptomatic hypokalemia and its cardiac/ECG risk (and the digoxin-toxicity danger) and ensure cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — The cardiac threat (arrhythmia, digoxin toxicity) is the immediate danger to detect and monitor. RN role: recognize + monitor the heart.

opts.text: Complete pre-administration safety checks — verify the order/level, assess renal function/urine output, check magnesium, and obtain a baseline ECG
opts.rat: #2 — These safeguards must precede administration to prevent hyperkalemia and ensure effective replacement. RN role: pre-administration assessment.

opts.text: Administer the IV potassium SAFELY — premixed/diluted, by pump, within rate/route limits, never pushed — with an independent double-check
opts.rat: #3 — Safe administration of the high-alert drug prevents fatal errors. RN role: safe administration + double-check.

opts.text: Monitor during/after — ECG, IV site, and a recheck of potassium — and prevent over-correction
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing monitoring catches arrhythmia, site reactions, and over-correction. RN role: monitor + prevent over-correction.

opts.text: Educate the patient on dietary potassium, follow-up labs, and reporting symptoms
opts.rat: #5 — Education sustains correction and prevents recurrence. RN role: patient teaching.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for safe potassium replacement.

opts.text: Verify the order and serum level, assess renal function and urine output, check magnesium, and obtain a baseline ECG before administering
opts.rat: Essential pre-administration safeguards. RN role: complete safety checks.

opts.text: Use a commercially premixed, diluted potassium bag and infuse it via an infusion pump
opts.rat: Safe formulation and controlled delivery. RN role: premixed + pump.

opts.text: Limit a peripheral infusion to about 10 mEq/hr; use a central line with continuous cardiac monitoring for faster rates
opts.rat: Correct rate/route limits protect veins and the heart. RN role: respect rate/route limits.

opts.text: Perform an independent double-check of the high-alert medication (drug, concentration, rate, pump) with barcode scanning
opts.rat: High-alert medication safeguard. RN role: independent double-check.

opts.text: Monitor the ECG, assess the IV site frequently (slow/stop for pain or phlebitis), recheck potassium, and watch for over-correction
opts.rat: Appropriate during/after monitoring. RN role: monitor + prevent over-correction.

opts.text: Use oral potassium instead when the deficit is mild and the patient can tolerate it
opts.rat: Oral is the preferred, safer route when feasible. RN role: choose the safest effective route.

opts.text: Give a rapid IV potassium push/bolus to correct the level quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG and potentially fatal — IV potassium is never pushed; rapid bolus causes cardiac arrest. Always dilute and infuse by pump.

opts.text: Add concentrated potassium to the patient's already-hanging IV bag at the bedside
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — risks an inadvertent bolus/uneven concentration; concentrated KCl is off floor stock and premixed bags are used.

opts.text: Administer potassium without confirming adequate renal function or urine output
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — impaired kidneys/oliguria greatly raise the risk of dangerous hyperkalemia; confirm renal function/urine output first.

opts.text: Increase the infusion rate beyond the order or run a high rate through a peripheral line without monitoring to 'catch up'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — exceeding the rate or high peripheral rates without central access/monitoring risks arrhythmia and severe vein injury.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize the symptomatic hypokalemia and cardiac risk, place the patient on a cardiac monitor, and obtain a baseline ECG
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Establishing the cardiac risk and monitoring frames safe replacement. RN role: recognize + monitor.

opts.text: Complete pre-administration checks — verify the order/level, confirm renal function/urine output, and check/replace magnesium
opts.rat: #2 — Second. These safeguards must precede giving potassium. RN role: pre-administration assessment.

opts.text: Perform the independent high-alert double-check, then administer the premixed/diluted potassium by pump within rate/route limits (never push)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Safe administration with verification prevents fatal errors. RN role: double-check + safe administration.

opts.text: Monitor the ECG and IV site during the infusion and recheck the potassium afterward, watching for over-correction
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Ongoing monitoring detects arrhythmia, site reactions, and hyperkalemia. RN role: monitor + recheck.

opts.text: Educate the patient on dietary potassium, follow-up labs, and reporting symptoms; document
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Education and documentation sustain safe correction. RN role: teach + document.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse confirmed adequate urine output and renal function and checked/replaced magnesium before giving potassium
opts.rat: Renal/urine confirmation and magnesium correction are essential pre-administration safeguards. Correct practice.

opts.text: A commercially premixed, diluted potassium bag was infused by pump at a peripheral-appropriate rate (~10 mEq/hr) with telemetry
opts.rat: Safe formulation, controlled delivery, and rate/route compliance. Correct practice.

opts.text: An independent double-check of the high-alert medication and barcode scanning were performed before administration
opts.rat: High-alert medication safeguards prevent errors. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse monitored the ECG and IV site, rechecked the potassium, and watched for over-correction to hyperkalemia
opts.rat: Appropriate during/after monitoring. Correct practice.

opts.text: When the IV site became painful and reddened, the nurse slowed/stopped the infusion and reassessed access
opts.rat: Potassium is a vein irritant; responding to site reaction protects the patient. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse gave a rapid IV potassium push to correct the dangerously low level quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ Potentially fatal — IV potassium is never pushed; rapid bolus causes cardiac arrest. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse added concentrated potassium to the hanging IV bag at the bedside to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ Risks an inadvertent bolus and uneven concentration; premixed bags must be used. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse increased the infusion well beyond the ordered rate through a peripheral line to 'catch up'
opts.rat: ⚠️ Exceeding the rate (and high peripheral rates without central access/monitoring) risks arrhythmia and vein injury. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse administered the potassium despite the patient being oliguric with rising creatinine, without notifying the provider
opts.rat: ⚠️ Impaired renal function/oliguria contraindicates routine potassium and risks lethal hyperkalemia; the provider must be notified. Improper practice.

opts.text: After correction, the nurse taught the patient about dietary potassium and the need for follow-up labs while on diuretics
opts.rat: Education sustains correction and prevents recurrence. Correct practice.
